title: Golden Hour
description: Portraits and landscapes shot in the last hour of daylight.
aiPrompt: You are writing captions for a photography portfolio. For each photo in the 'Golden Hour' series (portraits and landscapes in the last hour of daylight), write a short poetic caption of exactly 2 sentences. Tone: warm, luminous, slightly nostalgic. Return a JSON array of strings, one per photo, in order.

photos.caption: She turned away from the sun and the sun refused to leave her. In that hour, the light forgot to be light and became something else entirely.
The field swallowed the figure slowly, the way all warm things disappear. What remained was the outline of a question the horizon had already answered.
The sea caught fire for exactly seven minutes and no one could explain it. The rocks have held the memory since, smooth and patient and burning.
Light arrived sideways that evening, the way truth sometimes does. Everything it touched became temporary and precious at once.
The last twenty minutes of sun are a different country entirely. She walked through it without knowing she would never return.
Even the shadows were warm that day, which is something you only notice once it's gone. The camera caught it; the memory already had.

title: Midnight City
description: Long-exposure urban photography shot between midnight and 4am.
aiPrompt: You are writing captions for a photography portfolio. For each photo in the 'Midnight City' series (long-exposure urban photography between midnight and 4am), write a short poetic caption of exactly 2 sentences. Tone: electric, lonely, quietly beautiful. Return a JSON array of strings, one per photo, in order.

photos.caption: The rain left a second city beneath the first, brighter and more honest. Every car that passed rewrote it, and every car was forgiven.
Open all night means something different at 3am — it means someone stayed. The sign hums in a key no instrument claims.
Four directions with no one to choose between them, the traffic light cycles anyway. Red, green, red — a conversation with an absent city.
At 2am the city stops performing and just exists. These lights were never meant to be beautiful; that is exactly why they are.
The exposure was thirty seconds but the feeling lasted longer. Something about the stillness of a moving city.
Streets remember every foot that ever crossed them; at night they finally have room to think. The pavement was wet with something other than rain.
There is a particular loneliness in neon that daylight cannot replicate. This corner held it perfectly, without trying.
The last frame of the night is always the truest. By then the city has forgotten you are watching.
